title: Closures
summary: A closure gives a function access to variables from its outer (enclosing) lexical scope, even after the outer function returns.
content:
## What is a closure?

A **closure** is created when a nested function captures variables from its parent scope. Those variables persist as long as any reference to the inner function exists.

## Why it matters

Closures power modules before ES modules, memoization, React hooks (conceptually), and event handlers that remember configuration.

## Common pitfalls

- Accidentally sharing loop variables in older `var` loops (use `let` or IIFE).
- Memory: closures keep outer variables alive—detach listeners when done.

title: Promises and async/await
summary: Promises model asynchronous completion. async/await is syntactic sugar that makes promise chains read like synchronous code.
content:
## Promises

A Promise is an object representing eventual completion (`resolve`) or failure (`reject`).

## async/await

`async` functions always return a Promise. `await` pauses until the Promise settles *within that async function only*—it does not block the main thread globally.

## Error handling

Use `try/catch` around `await`, or `.catch()` on the promise chain.
